content.text: Third-party API integration is how agents interact with the external world — payment processors, email services, weather data, mapping, CRM systems, and more. The core challenge is the same regardless of the provider: translate a model's tool call into an authenticated HTTP request, handle every failure mode that the external service can produce, and return only the fields the model needs.

attrs.alt: Third-party API integration request lifecycle diagram
attrs.title: Third-Party API Request Lifecycle

content.text: Three things define a robust third-party integration: the API key never appears in the tool schema; errors from the external service map to your structured error vocabulary; and only the fields the model needs come back in the result.

per provider at module load time. Attach the Authorization header and configure retry logic in the adapter — every tool handler in that module shares the same session, which avoids repeated TCP handshakes and keeps connection-pool configuration in one place.

import os
import requests
from requests.adapters import HTTPAdapter
from urllib3.util.retry import Retry

_STRIPE_BASE = "https://api.stripe.com/v1"

def _make_stripe_session() -> requests.Session:
    session = requests.Session()
    api_key = os.environ["STRIPE_SECRET_KEY"]  # raises KeyError if missing
    session.auth = (api_key, "")               # Stripe uses HTTP Basic with key as user
    adapter = HTTPAdapter(
        max_retries=Retry(
            total=3,
            backoff_factor=0.5,
            status_forcelist=[500, 502, 503, 504],
            allowed_methods=["GET", "POST"],
        ),
        pool_connections=4,
        pool_maxsize=8,
    )
    session.mount("https://", adapter)
    return session

_STRIPE = _make_stripe_session()
The session is built once when the module is imported. If
is missing from the environment, the process crashes at startup — a deliberate fail-fast that surfaces missing configuration immediately rather than at the first tool call.
Every third-party API has its own error vocabulary. Map it to your internal vocabulary once, in a helper, so the rest of your handlers never branch on HTTP status codes directly.
field and can choose to wait or escalate. It never has to reason about HTTP status codes.
Read Tool: Fetching a Stripe Customer
A read tool retrieves data and prunes the response to exactly what the model needs. Stripe's customer object has dozens of fields — the model almost never needs more than a handful.

The raw Stripe customer object can contain 40+ fields including full address, metadata dictionaries, and nested payment method objects. Returning all of it would bloat the model's context; returning only what it needs keeps tool results compact and clear.
Write Tool: Creating a Payment Intent

Write tools that mutate data require careful validation before the request is sent. For payments, an out-of-range amount or missing currency produces an ambiguous Stripe error; catching it locally and returning
before making the request is cleaner.
. This detail is specific to Stripe; always read a provider's docs rather than assuming JSON.

The HTTPAdapter retry handles transient server errors (5xx) automatically. But 429 Too Many Requests is different: the provider wants you to wait a specific duration before retrying, not use an exponential backoff. Handle it explicitly.

import time

def _request_with_rate_limit(
    session: requests.Session,
    method: str,
    url: str,
    **kwargs,
) -> requests.Response:
    """Retry once after a 429, honoring the Retry-After header."""
    resp = session.request(method, url, **kwargs)
    if resp.status_code == 429:
        retry_after = int(resp.headers.get("Retry-After", 1))
        if retry_after > 30:        # refuse to block a tool call for >30s
            return resp             # let caller return RATE_LIMITED immediately
        time.sleep(retry_after)
        resp = session.request(method, url, **kwargs)
    return resp
The 30-second guard matters: blocking a tool call for longer than that stalls the entire agentic loop. If the provider's
to the model and let it decide — it might tell the user to try again later.
Many third-party APIs paginate results using a cursor or page token. Return the cursor to the model so it can fetch the next page in a subsequent tool call.
when more pages exist. It can then call
Never call live APIs in tests. Use
methods with a fixture that returns a pre-built response object.

import pytest
from unittest.mock import MagicMock, patch
from mymodule import get_stripe_customer, create_payment_intent

def _mock_resp(status: int, body: dict) -> MagicMock:
    r = MagicMock()
    r.status_code = status
    r.ok = status < 400
    r.json.return_value = body
    r.text = str(body)
    r.headers = {}
    return r

def test_get_customer_success():
    customer_payload = {
        "id": "cus_abc",
        "email": "alice@example.com",
        "name": "Alice",
        "currency": "usd",
        "created": 1700000000,
        "delinquent": False,
        "subscriptions": {"data": []},
    }
    with patch("mymodule._STRIPE") as mock_session:
        mock_session.get.return_value = _mock_resp(200, customer_payload)
        result = get_stripe_customer("cus_abc")
    assert result["email"] == "alice@example.com"
    assert "subscriptions" in result

def test_get_customer_not_found():
    err_payload = {"error": {"message": "No such customer", "code": "resource_missing"}}
    with patch("mymodule._STRIPE") as mock_session:
        mock_session.get.return_value = _mock_resp(404, err_payload)
        result = get_stripe_customer("cus_missing")
    assert result["error"] == "NOT_FOUND"

def test_create_payment_intent_below_minimum():
    result = create_payment_intent(amount_cents=10, currency="usd")
    assert result["error"] == "VALIDATION_ERROR"
    assert "50" in result["message"]

def test_create_payment_intent_rate_limited():
    rate_limit_resp = MagicMock()
    rate_limit_resp.status_code = 429
    rate_limit_resp.ok = False
    rate_limit_resp.headers = {"Retry-After": "5"}
    rate_limit_resp.json.return_value = {"error": {"message": "Too many requests"}}
    rate_limit_resp.text = ""
    with patch("mymodule._STRIPE") as mock_session:
        mock_session.post.return_value = rate_limit_resp
        result = create_payment_intent(amount_cents=500, currency="usd")
    assert result["error"] == "RATE_LIMITED"
    assert "retry_after_seconds" in result
Third-party API integrations need more operational discipline than internal tools because you do not control the external service.
A single hung request blocks an agent loop indefinitely. Set
(3s connect, 10s read) for most APIs; increase for batch/export endpoints.
Most providers include a request ID in response headers (
). Log it on errors to enable provider-side debugging.
Payment and messaging APIs often support an idempotency key (
header on Stripe) that makes retried requests safe. Generate a UUID per tool call and include it.
If your agent acts on events pushed by the provider (payment settled, email bounced), verify the webhook signature before processing. Stripe provides

If the model fetches the same customer or product repeatedly in a single conversation, cache the result with a short TTL (30–60 seconds) keyed on the resource ID. This avoids redundant external calls without serving stale data.
Tool call hangs indefinitely on slow API
Model context bloated with unused fields
Prune to needed fields in the handler
Creating a new session per call
TCP handshake overhead on every tool call
Key appears in model's context window
Keys live in env vars, injected in handler only
Rapid retry hammers the rate limit
Read header, sleep ≤30s or surface RATE_LIMITED
per provider; configure auth and retry in the adapter at startup.
Map provider-specific HTTP status codes to your structured error vocabulary (NOT_FOUND, PERMISSION_DENIED, RATE_LIMITED, CONFLICT, SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE) in a single helper.
Prune every API response to only the fields the model needs before returning.
, sleeping if ≤30s, or returning RATE_LIMITED so the model can decide.
Return pagination cursors so the model can fetch subsequent pages by calling the tool again.
Test with mocked sessions rather than live API calls; verify error path behavior, not just the happy path.
